claim: Chronic aerobic exercise alters endothelial function, impairing vasodilating mechanisms mediated by NO.

result.claim: Chronic aerobic exercise alters endothelial function, impairing vasodilating mechanisms mediated by NO.
result.reasoning:
The claim that chronic aerobic exercise alters endothelial function, impairing vasodilating mechanisms mediated by NO, is strongly refuted by the evidence. Multiple propositions from two different papers indicate that exercise training enhances, rather than impairs, vasodilation and nitric oxide production.

Paper 10697096, "Vasodilator responses of coronary resistance arteries of exercise-trained pigs," explicitly states that "Exercise training enhances vasodilation through increased endothelium-derived relaxing factor/nitric oxide production" (prop_2353) and that "The L-arginine/nitric oxide synthase pathway is involved in exercise training induced vasodilation" (prop_2354). It also notes that "Exercise training enhances bradykinin-induced vasodilation" (prop_2352, prop_2350).

Similarly, Paper 1122279, "Endothelium-mediated relaxation of porcine collateral-dependent arterioles is improved by exercise training," supports this by stating that "Exercise training increases endothelial nitric oxide synthase (ecNOS) mRNA levels of collateral-dependent microvasculature" (prop_707) and "Exercise training enhances endothelium-mediated relaxation of collateral-dependent microvasculature" (prop_706). Furthermore, it highlights that "After training, ecNOS mRNA expression was not different between LAD and LCx arterioles" (prop_716, prop_718), indicating an improvement in NO-related mechanisms in exercise-trained animals compared to sedentary ones where ecNOS mRNA expression was decreased (prop_715, prop_717).

Both papers consistently demonstrate that chronic aerobic exercise leads to an improvement in NO-mediated vasodilation, directly contradicting the claim.
